title: Hikvision IP Camera Improper Authentication CISA KEV: HiatusRAT Threat Actor Exploits CVE-2017-7921 Nine-Year-Old Authentication Bypass for Mass IoT Surveillance Network Infiltration

timeline.event: CVE-2017-7921 discovered in Hikvision IP camera firmware; improper authentication via ISAPI URL parameters
Hikvision issues official security advisory and limited firmware patch for select models
Public exploit code (PoC) published on Exploit-DB, dramatically increasing exploitation risk
FBI warns of HiatusRAT actors conducting wide-scale scanning campaign targeting CVE-2017-7921 across Five Eyes countries (US, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, UK)
SANS Internet Storm Center reports renewed wave of mass exploit attempts; honeypot sensors record thousands of daily attacks
CISA adds CVE-2017-7921 to Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog with federal remediation deadline of March 26, 2026

simple_summary.what_happened: A 9-year-old vulnerability in Hikvision security cameras was officially confirmed as actively exploited and added to CISA's must-patch list on March 5, 2026. The flaw allows anyone on the internet to access Hikvision cameras without a password — just by sending a specially crafted web request. Millions of these cameras are deployed worldwide in homes, businesses, and critical infrastructure, and many remain unpatched.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations and individuals using Hikvision IP cameras and DVRs with firmware versions 5.2.0 through 5.3.9, specifically the DS-2CD, DS-2DF, DS-2CD63xx, and DS-2DFx series. Federal agencies must patch by March 26, 2026. Privately owned businesses, manufacturing facilities, retail sites, and home users with internet-exposed cameras are all at risk.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attackers use automated scanning tools like Shodan or specialized IoT scanners to identify Hikvision cameras exposed to the internet. Step 2: The attacker sends a crafted HTTP GET request to the camera's ISAPI endpoint (e.g., /System/deviceInfo?auth=YWRtaW46MTEK) with base64-encoded default credentials embedded in the URL parameter. Step 3: The camera's firmware incorrectly processes this request and responds with full device configuration, credentials, and network settings without requiring proper authentication. Step 4: Using the obtained credentials and admin access, the attacker can modify camera settings, access live/recorded video feeds, extract stored credentials, and pivot into the internal network. Step 5: The attacker installs a backdoor or persistence mechanism (such as HiatusRAT malware) and uses the compromised camera as a foothold for lateral movement within the target organization's network.
what_you_should_do: Immediately inventory all Hikvision camera and DVR devices across your network and identify firmware versions
Isolate all Hikvision cameras into a separate VLAN and block internet-facing access to camera management ports (80, 8080, 8000, 8081)
Apply Hikvision's available firmware patches for affected models, or plan device replacement if patches are unavailable
Change all default credentials on every Hikvision device and disable Telnet access
Deploy IDS/IPS rules for the specific Hikvision authentication bypass pattern (look for /System/deviceInfo?auth= in HTTP logs)
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: This vulnerability requires zero authentication and zero user interaction — any attacker who can reach the camera over the network can fully compromise it in seconds. With millions of Hikvision cameras deployed globally and public exploit tools freely available, the attack barrier is extremely low. The HiatusRAT campaign has specifically targeted these cameras to build botnets and gain persistent network footholds in critical organizations. Compromised cameras can serve as pivots for ransomware deployment, espionage, or physical security failures.

tactics.description: Attackers exploit the unauthenticated ISAPI endpoint on internet-facing Hikvision cameras to gain unauthorized administrative access without any credentials
Threat actors use automated scanning tools including Ingram and Shodan queries to identify vulnerable Hikvision cameras across large IP ranges
HiatusRAT operators combine CVE-2017-7921 exploitation with Medusa brute-force tool to extract and test camera credentials
Attackers deploy persistent backdoors and web shells on compromised cameras to maintain access for ongoing operations
Compromised cameras serve as network footholds enabling attackers to transfer tools and move laterally into internal organizational networks

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on monitoring network traffic to camera ISAPI endpoints, identifying authentication bypass patterns in web server logs, and monitoring for unusual outbound connections from camera IP addresses. Firewall and IDS rules targeting the specific URL pattern are the most reliable detection method.

log_sources.description: Monitor for HTTP/HTTPS requests to Hikvision camera management ports (80, 8080, 8000, 8081) from unauthorized source IP addresses, particularly looking for /System/deviceInfo and /ISAPI/ endpoint access

log_sources.source: Hikvision Camera Web Server Access Logs
log_sources.description: Review built-in access logs for requests to /System/deviceInfo endpoint with auth= URL parameters, and flag any successful responses containing device configuration data
Analyze outbound network connections from camera IP addresses to external destinations, which may indicate backdoor C2 communication after compromise
Fortinet FortiGuard maintains signature 'Hikvision.DS.CVE-2017-7921.Authentication.Bypass'; deploy and monitor for triggered alerts

what_to_look_for.indicator: Authentication bypass URL pattern in HTTP logs
what_to_look_for.description: Any HTTP request containing /System/deviceInfo?auth= indicates an active exploitation attempt of CVE-2017-7921

what_to_look_for.indicator: ISAPI endpoint access from external IP
what_to_look_for.description: Requests to /ISAPI/ endpoints from IP addresses outside the organization's trusted engineering subnet indicate potential unauthorized access
what_to_look_for.log_field: http.request.uri CONTAINS '/ISAPI/' AND src.ip NOT IN trusted_subnets

what_to_look_for.indicator: Outbound connections from camera IPs to external destinations
what_to_look_for.description: Cameras initiating connections to external IP addresses on non-standard ports may indicate post-compromise HiatusRAT C2 activity
what_to_look_for.log_field: src.ip IN camera_inventory AND dst.ip NOT IN trusted_management_ips

what_to_look_for.indicator: Telnet access to camera management ports
what_to_look_for.description: Telnet connections (port 23) to camera devices from any source suggest active unauthorized administration attempts

immediate_actions.action: Network Isolation - Segment All Hikvision Cameras
immediate_actions.description: Immediately place all Hikvision IP cameras and DVRs into an isolated VLAN with no internet access. Block external access to camera management ports (TCP 80, 8080, 8000, 8081, 443) from the internet. This is the single most effective short-term mitigation.

Run an immediate inventory of all Hikvision devices on the network. Identify devices running firmware versions 5.2.0 through 5.3.9. Prioritize patching or replacing devices with vulnerable firmware. Check https://www.hikvision.com/us-en/support/document-center/special-notices/ for available firmware updates.

Reset administrator passwords on every Hikvision device. Use strong, unique passwords per device. Disable the default 'admin' account where possible. Disable Telnet access and enable only HTTPS for management.

immediate_actions.action: Deploy Detection Rules for CVE-2017-7921 Exploitation
immediate_actions.description: Configure IDS/IPS with Hikvision authentication bypass signature. Add firewall log alert for any HTTP request containing '/System/deviceInfo?auth=' pattern. Review historical logs for evidence of prior exploitation attempts.

immediate_actions.action: Apply Firmware Patches or Plan Device Replacement by March 26, 2026
immediate_actions.description: Federal agencies and organizations under BOD 22-01 must remediate by March 26, 2026. For devices without available patches, initiate procurement for replacement devices from a patched firmware baseline. Document remediation steps taken.
